lead.headline: US strikes Iran after drone attack on cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz
lead.summary: The US military struck Iranian missile storage facilities, drone bases, and coastal radar positions on Friday after a drone hit a cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz. President Trump called the attack a ceasefire violation. US Central Command confirmed strikes on multiple targets inside Iran within 24 hours of the cargo ship incident.
lead.takeaway: The US-Iran ceasefire is thread-thin: one drone on a ship in the world's most critical oil corridor triggered military strikes in under a day.
lead.why: The Strait of Hormuz handles roughly 20% of global seaborne oil, and India is one of its largest dependent importers. Any sustained disruption raises crude import bills, pressures the rupee, and resets refinery margins. Even a brief spike puts India's fuel subsidy arithmetic under stress. For anyone thinking about energy strategy or macroeconomics with an India lens, keep the Strait on a short-refresh watchlist this weekend.

frontpage.headline: OpenAI, SpaceX and others race to build their own chips and shrink Nvidia's grip
frontpage.summary: OpenAI has unveiled Jalapeño, a custom inference chip being designed with TSMC, as part of a wave of Big Tech firms building in-house silicon to reduce dependence on Nvidia GPUs. Google, Amazon, Meta and SpaceX are all on similar paths, collectively compressing Nvidia's pricing power on the inference side of the AI stack.
frontpage.takeaway: The inference chip market is fracturing fast — Nvidia's monopoly on AI hardware is being dissolved from below.
frontpage.why: Cheaper inference changes the economics of running AI products dramatically. If you are building on top of AI or advising companies that do, factor in a sustained drop in API costs over the next two to three years. India's semiconductor ambitions also get a clearer target: inference chips may be a more achievable wedge than training silicon.

frontpage.headline: OpenAI appoints Uber India chief as its India head, doubling down on its second-largest market
frontpage.summary: Prabhjeet Singh, who ran Uber India for several years, joins OpenAI as Managing Director India. OpenAI has been expanding offices, partnerships and hiring in India rapidly, and this hire is the clearest signal yet that the company treats India as a strategic market requiring a dedicated local operator.
frontpage.takeaway: OpenAI is no longer content to let India grow passively — it is hiring a local heavyweight to own the market.
frontpage.why: For someone pivoting toward AI-generalist or founder's-office roles, the arrival of a serious India MD means enterprise AI deals and government partnerships will move faster. Singh's background is consumer and operations, not pure tech — expect OpenAI's India push to be commercially aggressive, not just developer-oriented.

frontpage.headline: Top global leaders to attend India-AI Impact Summit in New Delhi next week
frontpage.summary: An AI Impact Summit bringing together heads of state and global tech leaders is scheduled in New Delhi in early July. India is positioning the event as a declaration of its role in shaping the global AI agenda, not just adopting it.
frontpage.takeaway: India is moving from AI importer to AI agenda-setter — New Delhi next week is where that story gets written.
frontpage.why: Policy announcements from this summit will likely set the direction of India's AI regulatory posture, public procurement priorities, and global partnership terms for the next two to three years. If you are tracking where to position in the Indian AI ecosystem, the summit's outcomes are worth following closely.

frontpage.headline: Climate science confirms human emissions supercharged Europe's second record heatwave in two months
frontpage.summary: Western and central Europe has broken heat records for the second time in eight weeks. Attribution scientists confirm that human-caused warming made this heatwave substantially more intense and more probable. The UK broke its June temperature record for three consecutive days; France postponed public events; Poland issued wildfire warnings.
frontpage.takeaway: Back-to-back attribution-confirmed records mean Europe's unprecedented heat is now the baseline, not the exception.
frontpage.why: India has direct exposure: European logistics slowdowns affect Indian exporters; climate migration from affected regions will reshape geopolitics; and rapid attribution science is tightening legal liability on emitters worldwide. Useful context for anyone thinking about climate risk in business strategy.

frontpage.headline: IMF warns AI will fuel inflation not just through chip costs but by making consumers richer
frontpage.summary: The IMF's chief economist argues that AI will drive inflation via a demand channel that has been under-discussed: by generating wealth, AI makes consumers more willing to spend, putting upward pressure on prices beyond the chip and energy cost spikes already in the forecasts. The report calls on central banks to update their models.
frontpage.takeaway: AI is inflationary in a second, subtler way that central banks have not fully priced in — the wealth effect.
frontpage.why: If this analysis holds, the AI drives deflation consensus narrative is incomplete. Rate cuts may come later than markets expect, in both the US and India. For anyone in macro or strategy, this IMF view is a useful contrarian data point worth stress-testing against your own assumptions.

frontpage.headline: India startup funding tops $1.09 billion this week, up 290% year-on-year
frontpage.summary: A CRED mega-round anchored an exceptional week for Indian startup funding, pushing the weekly total past $1 billion for the first time in years. Year-on-year the jump is 290%, though CRED's outsized round means the headline number flatters the broader ecosystem signal.
frontpage.takeaway: Adjust for the CRED effect, but a 290% year-on-year surge still signals serious capital returning to Indian tech.
frontpage.why: Consumer fintech at scale is back in favour with investors — CRED's raise is the clearest proof. The next two weeks of deal flow will show whether this is a one-off or the start of a fresh cycle. For someone building or advising in the ecosystem, this is a good moment to watch which sectors follow.

frontpage.headline: China's Zhipu AI delivers another low-cost frontier model, analysts call it a new DeepSeek moment
frontpage.summary: Beijing-based Zhipu AI has released a coding model competitive on benchmarks with Western equivalents at a fraction of the training cost, drawing comparisons to DeepSeek's January 2025 market shock. The model is attracting global developer attention.
frontpage.takeaway: China producing capable low-cost frontier models is no longer a one-off — it is becoming a structural pattern.
frontpage.why: Each new Chinese model release compresses the time before powerful AI is cheap and widely available. For Indian founders and builders, this expands the model menu beyond proprietary Western APIs. It also raises a genuine strategic question: should Indian AI products be built on commercial US APIs, or on increasingly capable open-weight alternatives from China?

frontpage.headline: White House asks OpenAI to delay its next model launch — and OpenAI complies
frontpage.summary: The Trump administration asked OpenAI to stagger the release of its next AI model, citing national security and geopolitical timing concerns. OpenAI adjusted its release schedule in response. This is reportedly the first time a US administration has directly intervened in a frontier AI model's commercial launch timing.
frontpage.takeaway: The US government now treats frontier AI releases as foreign policy events — product launches need a geopolitical clearance.
frontpage.why: If model releases become government-gated, the innovation rhythm for everyone building on top of frontier models changes. For Indian developers specifically, dependence on US-controlled model APIs carries a new kind of supply-chain risk — one more reason why China's open-weight push and India's own AI models program matter.

stories.headline: After Anthropic's Mythos suspension, India formally pushes for guaranteed AI model access
stories.summary: Following the temporary suspension of Anthropic's Mythos model to Indian users, India's government formally pushed the US for assurances of stable AI technology access. The US has responded with commitments, and Mythos is being made available to trusted partners in a staged release.
stories.takeaway: India has learned the hard way that US-controlled AI models can be switched off — and is now negotiating access guarantees.
stories.why: This is the geopolitics of AI dependency made concrete. India's AI ambitions — government, enterprise and startup — rest on models it does not control. The Mythos episode will likely accelerate India's push for domestic AI models and diversified vendor agreements. For anyone in AI policy or strategy, this is the most important structural story in Indian AI this month.

stories.headline: AI's biggest retail transformation is invisible to shoppers — it is all in the back office
stories.summary: MIT Technology Review argues that AI's most significant retail impact is not chatbot shopping assistants or virtual try-ons, but operational transformation: inventory management, demand forecasting, supplier negotiation, and shrinkage reduction. These back-end changes are already delivering measurable margin improvements.
stories.takeaway: In retail, AI is an operations story, not a consumer interface story — the ROI is in the warehouse, not the chatbot.
stories.why: For anyone advising retail or consumer businesses, this reframes where to look for AI value. The same pattern — unglamorous back-office adoption driving the real returns — likely applies across Indian retail, where operational efficiency gains can be enormous.

stories.headline: US allows Anthropic to release Mythos to trusted partners after India access dispute
stories.summary: After pressure from India and allied governments over the suspension of Anthropic's Claude Mythos 5, the US has permitted a staged release to trusted partners. The episode signals a new dynamic: AI model releases are now diplomatic events, not just product milestones.
stories.takeaway: Anthropic's Mythos is back, but only for vetted partners — open frontier model access may be quietly ending.
stories.why: Pair this with the White House's OpenAI delay request. A pattern is forming: the US government is treating frontier AI access as a tool of influence, rationed by trust. For India, building on US-hosted models now carries diplomatic as well as technical risk.

stories.headline: China gives AI agents digital ID cards under new national standards
stories.summary: China's State Administration has released national standards requiring AI agents to be registered with a unified identity system — essentially digital ID cards for autonomous AI. The move is aimed at accountability and traceability as AI agents take on more autonomous roles in business and government.
stories.takeaway: China is building regulatory infrastructure for AI agents before the West has even agreed on what they are.
stories.why: As AI agents proliferate in India's enterprise and government sectors, the question of identity, accountability and auditability will come up. China's approach — mandatory identity, centralised registration — is likely to inform global debates. Worth tracking if you are building or advising in the AI-agents space.

stories.headline: India emerges as a global semiconductor GCC hub, Capgemini analysis finds
stories.summary: A Capgemini report positions India as an increasingly significant global capability centre for semiconductor design and engineering. The combination of engineering talent, government incentives, and growing fab ambitions is making India a serious location for chip R&D alongside Taiwan, the US and the Netherlands.
stories.takeaway: India's semiconductor story is becoming a design and engineering story, not just a fab-subsidy story.
stories.why: GCC-led semiconductor work builds genuine IP and talent depth, not just assembly lines. It also positions India better for the inference-chip wave discussed in today's AI section. Keep an eye on which multinationals are setting up or expanding semiconductor GCCs in India.

stories.headline: Apple raises iPad and MacBook prices, citing AI-era chip cost surge
stories.summary: Apple raised prices on iPad and MacBook lines, citing the rising cost of memory and storage chips driven by AI infrastructure demand. The company warned that iPhone price hikes could follow. This is the first time Apple has publicly cited AI-related chip costs as a reason for consumer product price increases.
stories.takeaway: AI's chip cost surge is hitting consumer hardware prices — Apple just became the canary in this coal mine.
stories.why: For Indian consumers and enterprise buyers, this means premium hardware gets more expensive in dollar terms, and rupee depreciation amplifies the hit. It is also a concrete data point for the IMF's inflation thesis in today's economics section.

stories.headline: India can avoid $28 billion in climate losses with just 2% more resilience investment
stories.summary: A new report finds that India could avert an estimated $28 billion in renewable energy-related climate losses by investing approximately 2% more in resilience infrastructure — grid hardening, flood-resistant installations, and heat-tolerant design. Without this, climate damage to renewable assets will compound rapidly.
stories.takeaway: A 2% resilience premium on renewable projects avoids an eighteen-times-larger climate loss — the numbers are hard to argue with.
stories.why: This frames climate adaptation not as a cost but as an investment with a clear return. For anyone working in energy policy, infrastructure finance, or climate strategy, this report is a useful quantified anchor. It also points to an underserved market: climate-resilient renewable project design and insurance.

stories.headline: Indian state designates five new renewable energy zones to fast-track green projects
stories.summary: An Indian state government has created five new designated renewable energy zones designed to streamline permitting, land acquisition and grid connectivity for solar and wind projects. The move is part of a broader push to accelerate India's renewable capacity additions ahead of its 2030 targets.
stories.takeaway: Zone-based permitting is how India actually speeds up renewable energy — watch whether other states follow this template.
stories.why: The regulatory friction point in Indian renewables has always been land and grid access, not capital. Zone designations directly address that. If this model works, it is a template for other states. For anyone in energy or infrastructure, the replicability of this approach is the story.

stories.headline: Record European heat, London Climate Action Week, and Project Cosmos debut in the same week
stories.summary: Carbon Brief's weekly digest covers record-breaking heat across Europe, the opening of London Climate Action Week drawing global policy attention, and the debut of Project Cosmos — a new international climate science initiative. The convergence of extreme weather and policy events makes this a high-signal climate week globally.
stories.takeaway: London Climate Action Week coinciding with Europe's worst heat in decades may be the most politically potent climate moment of 2026 so far.
stories.why: When extreme weather events align with major policy gatherings, the political pressure for action spikes. Watch for announcements coming out of London this week that may affect India's climate commitments and finance flows.

stories.headline: India's pharma industry is tariff-proof but faces a deeper China dependency problem
stories.summary: An analysis in The Diplomat argues that India's generic pharma sector has successfully positioned itself beyond US tariff risk, but remains acutely dependent on Chinese active pharmaceutical ingredients. The piece traces how this dependency emerged and why substituting it is harder than Indian manufacturers admit.
stories.takeaway: India's pharma resilience story has a hidden vulnerability: the supply chain runs through China, not around it.
stories.why: For anyone tracking India's industrial strategy or healthcare supply chains, this is the honest version of the India as global pharmacy narrative. The API dependency is a strategic risk that both government and investors are underweighting. It is also an opportunity: whoever solves domestic API manufacturing in India at scale builds a genuinely strategic asset.

stories.headline: CRISPR gene editing crosses from laboratory promise into clinical reality
stories.summary: A new analysis in GEN reviews the progress of CRISPR-based therapies moving into clinical trials and early approvals. Several treatments for blood disorders, certain cancers and genetic conditions are now in advanced trials, with the first commercially approved CRISPR therapy already on the market in the US.
stories.takeaway: CRISPR is crossing the threshold from research tool to clinical medicine — the era of gene-editing treatments has started.
stories.why: India's CRISPR research base is growing, and the transition to clinical reality opens both a market opportunity in affordable gene therapy manufacturing and a policy challenge — regulatory frameworks for germline editing are still thin globally. Useful context for anyone tracking biotech or health investment.

stories.headline: Cold chain infrastructure is the missing link holding back India's next food economy
stories.summary: An ET Hospitality analysis argues that India's food economy is constrained less by production and more by the absence of integrated cold chain infrastructure. Post-harvest losses remain among the world's highest, limiting the value farmers can capture and the products India can export at quality.
stories.takeaway: India wastes roughly 16-18% of food post-harvest — cold chain is not a logistics problem, it is an economic opportunity.
stories.why: For anyone in agritech, food, or infrastructure, cold chain is one of the highest-leverage gaps in the Indian food system. The constraint is not capital scarcity but fragmented, unconnected facilities. A platform that integrates cold storage, transport and last-mile delivery at district level could unlock enormous value. Practically actionable for a founder.

stories.headline: FAO awards Modi its Agricola Medal for food security and sustainable agriculture
stories.summary: The UN's Food and Agriculture Organisation has awarded its Agricola Medal to Prime Minister Narendra Modi, recognising India's advances in food security, digital agriculture tools, and sustainable farming initiatives. India has moved from chronic food deficit to agricultural exporter over the past decade.
stories.takeaway: The FAO recognition is diplomatic, but India's agricultural transformation of the past decade is real and underreported.
stories.why: For agritech founders, the headline signals continued government openness to agricultural innovation — grants, policy support and procurement are easier to access when the political signal is favourable. Useful context on where India's soft power and government priority is being directed globally.

stories.headline: Hyundai and TVS launch EV ride-hailing app Blue Move in Delhi NCR
stories.summary: Hyundai Motor and TVS Motor have jointly launched Blue Move, an electric vehicle ride-hailing platform piloting in Delhi NCR. The launch follows Vietnamese EV maker VinFast's recent entry into the same space, indicating a wave of OEM-led EV mobility platforms challenging Ola and Uber.
stories.takeaway: OEMs are going direct-to-rider in Indian EV mobility — the platform layer is up for grabs.
stories.why: When hardware manufacturers build their own platforms, it usually signals that they do not trust pure-play aggregators to sell or sustain their vehicles at the required volume. For Indian mobility startups and investors, Blue Move signals that the OEM-platform model is becoming viable here. Watch whether aggregators or OEM-led platforms win urban EV mobility over the next 18 months.

stories.headline: India drops 20% local sourcing rule for satellite broadband, opens market with security guardrails
stories.summary: India has removed the requirement that satellite broadband operators source 20% of their components locally, a rule that had blocked market entry for Starlink, Eutelsat OneWeb and others. Security guardrails and data localisation requirements remain in place. The move effectively opens India's satcom market to global players.
stories.takeaway: India has chosen market openness over local sourcing in satellite broadband — Starlink's path to India just got clearer.
stories.why: Satcom connectivity could transform rural internet access, agricultural information systems, and disaster response in India. For founders building in rural tech, logistics or agritech, affordable satellite connectivity in the next two to three years is a genuine infrastructure unlock worth building assumptions around.

stories.headline: RBI expands India's credit derivatives market with index derivatives and corporate bond swaps
stories.summary: The Reserve Bank of India has launched credit index derivatives and total return swaps on corporate bonds, expanding the toolkit available to institutional investors for managing credit risk. The move is part of a broader effort to deepen India's corporate bond market and attract foreign portfolio investors.
stories.takeaway: India's credit markets are maturing — RBI is adding institutional plumbing that most large economies already have.
stories.why: Deeper credit derivatives markets make it easier to price and transfer corporate credit risk, which in turn makes corporate bond markets more liquid and accessible. For founders thinking about financing options, and for investors in Indian debt, this is a slow-moving but important structural improvement.

stories.headline: S&P affirms US credit rating at AA+ with stable outlook despite high fiscal deficits
stories.summary: S&P Global Ratings maintained the US sovereign credit rating at AA+, one notch below the top triple-A, citing a resilient economy and high but stable fiscal deficits. The stable outlook signals S&P sees no near-term risk of a downgrade despite continued US government borrowing.
stories.takeaway: S&P is not alarmed by US deficits yet — but AA+ is already a downgrade from historic AAA, a reminder of the direction of travel.
stories.why: A stable US rating keeps dollar funding costs from spiking, which matters for emerging markets like India that price off US treasuries. Useful background context, not immediately actionable.

stories.headline: UPI and RuPay could become the spine of South Asian financial integration
stories.summary: An Observer Research Foundation analysis argues that India's digital payment infrastructure — UPI and RuPay — is the most practical tool available for deepening financial integration across South Asia. Cross-border UPI is already live in several countries; the piece maps what fuller regional adoption would require.
stories.takeaway: India's DPI stack is not just a domestic story — it is India's best tool for regional economic leadership.
stories.why: UPI's international expansion gives India soft power leverage that policy agreements cannot easily replicate. The read-through for founders: cross-border payment infrastructure in South Asia is becoming a real investable thesis as the rails go live country by country.

stories.headline: India-UK free trade agreement aims for transformational growth, 1,000 advisors deployed
stories.summary: Commerce Minister Piyush Goyal described the India-UK Comprehensive Economic and Trade Agreement as a vehicle for transformational growth for both economies. The government is deploying 1,000 advisory personnel and upgrading its trade portal to help Indian businesses leverage the deal.
stories.takeaway: India-UK CETA is signed — now comes the harder work of actually using it, which is where most FTAs fail.
stories.why: For Indian businesses and startups, the advisory infrastructure is the actionable angle: 1,000 trade advisors and a new portal mean tangible support for identifying UK market opportunities. If you are building anything with potential UK enterprise customers, this is the moment to explore what CETA actually offers your sector.

stories.headline: Japan PM Takaichi arrives in Delhi on July 1 for India-Japan annual summit
stories.summary: Japan Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi will visit New Delhi from July 1-3 for the annual India-Japan bilateral summit. The visit follows a change of venue from Assam to Delhi, and is expected to cover defence, semiconductors, green hydrogen, and economic partnerships.
stories.takeaway: Delhi is becoming the summit capital of mid-2026 — Japan this week, AI leaders next week — watch the MoUs on chips and green hydrogen.
stories.why: The India-Japan relationship is a consistent source of long-horizon industrial collaboration. Semiconductor and green hydrogen partnerships — both likely on the agenda — are sectors where India is actively trying to build domestic capability. Worth tracking the actual agreements signed.

stories.headline: India launches Operation Amistad to aid earthquake-hit Venezuela
stories.summary: India has dispatched a 41-member team of rescue personnel and medical professionals to Venezuela as part of Operation Amistad, following a significant earthquake. The team will work alongside Venezuelan authorities on search, rescue and medical relief operations.
stories.takeaway: India's humanitarian reach is extending to Latin America — modest in scale, significant in geography.
stories.why: India's growing willingness to project humanitarian capacity into Latin America signals an expanding foreign policy footprint beyond its traditional neighbourhoods. Useful context for understanding India's global positioning aspirations, not immediately actionable.

stories.headline: Israel and Lebanon sign a framework agreement, but Hezbollah is not party to it
stories.summary: Israel and Lebanon have signed a framework agreement following US-brokered negotiations, but Hezbollah is not a signatory. Previous ceasefires between the two countries have seen near-daily cross-border strikes despite formal agreements, raising questions about the framework's durability.
stories.takeaway: A Lebanon-Israel framework without Hezbollah's signature is a diplomatic document, not a ceasefire.
stories.why: The Levant's instability intersects with the Strait of Hormuz story in today's lead — any escalation pathway in the Middle East raises India's energy risk. Keep both stories in the same mental frame this weekend.

stories.headline: OECD confirms India remains the world's fastest-growing major economy
stories.summary: A new OECD report reaffirms India's position as the fastest-growing major economy, maintaining its growth trajectory even as global headwinds persist from trade tensions and AI-related inflation risks.
stories.takeaway: India's macro story is still the best available among large economies — and the OECD is now saying so explicitly.
stories.why: This puts India in a stronger negotiating position in trade and investment discussions — India-UK CETA, the Japan summit, the AI Impact Summit — when growth fundamentals are confirmed by multilateral bodies. For someone positioned toward India-focused work, useful validation.

stories.headline: Burkina Faso severs diplomatic ties with France, completing Francophone Africa's post-colonial break
stories.summary: Burkina Faso's ruling military junta has severed diplomatic relations with France, its former colonial power, accusing Paris of acting persistently against its interests. The move follows similar breaks by Mali and Niger and signals a broader reorientation of Francophone West Africa away from Paris.
stories.takeaway: France's postcolonial grip on West Africa is ending — and Russia, China, and increasingly India are filling the vacuum.
stories.why: As French influence retreats, India's Africa engagement — through the African Union, bilateral trade, and diaspora networks — becomes more strategically relevant. For someone interested in India's global positioning, the Sahel realignment is a trend worth tracking.

stories.headline: Severe heatwave puts 95 of the world's hottest cities inside India
stories.summary: A severe heatwave has placed 95 of the world's hottest cities within India, with cities across Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh and Madhya Pradesh recording extreme temperatures. Health and agricultural impacts are mounting across the affected regions.
stories.takeaway: When 95 of the world's hottest cities are in India simultaneously, the climate story is no longer abstract — it is the daily lived reality.
stories.why: This matters directly for a reader in Jaipur. Beyond the immediate health risk, sustained extreme heat reshapes labour productivity, agricultural yields, water availability and urban planning in ways that compound over years. The structural case for India's climate adaptation investment (see climate-energy-3) becomes harder to dispute the worse these numbers get.

stories.headline: Snow leopards, wolves and leopards share a Himalayan valley by eating different prey
stories.summary: A new study of a remote Nepal valley finds that three of Asia's most formidable apex predators — snow leopards, wolves and common leopards — coexist in overlapping territory not by avoiding each other in time or space, but by specialising in different prey species. Dietary partitioning, not territorial separation, is the mechanism.
stories.takeaway: Three apex predators sharing one valley — the coexistence trick is in what they eat, not where or when they hunt.
stories.why: A genuinely surprising result from the Himalayas. The dietary niche partitioning mechanism is also a useful mental model for competitive dynamics in markets where multiple strong players share territory. But mostly it is just a beautiful piece of science.

stories.headline: Claire North explains why she opened a space opera with a world-ending supernova 100 years away
stories.summary: Claire North, whose novel Slow Gods is the New Scientist Book Club's July pick, discusses the premise: a civilisation given 100 years of warning before their planet is destroyed. The essay explores how a society organises itself around certain doom and what it reveals about human nature, urgency, and denial.
stories.takeaway: A novel about 100 years of certain doom turns out to be about how humans handle knowledge of slow catastrophe — a theme very much of our age.
stories.why: Worth picking up if you like science fiction that takes seriously the psychology of long-horizon existential risk. The resonance with climate change, AI risk, and other slow-moving civilisational challenges is not accidental.

opportunities.name: First-Ever National Rural Development Conference
opportunities.summary: Inaugural national conference on rural development held in Delhi, bringing together policymakers, practitioners and researchers on India's rural economy. Relevant for anyone working on agritech, rural fintech, or India's development agenda. Delhi, national.

opportunities.name: SGSITS Incubation Forum Cohort 3.0 — Startup Grant Program
opportunities.summary: Third cohort of SGSITS Incubation Forum's startup grant program for early-stage ventures in India. Worth a look if you are early-stage or advising founders who are. National.
